Name: latency_optimized_quantization
Title: Latency-Optimized Quantization for Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a latency-optimized quantization approach can significantly reduce the inference latency of deep learning models on commodity GPUs, while maintaining accuracy, by leveraging kernel-level throughput engineering and hardware-faithful evaluation.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have focused on optimizing mixed-precision training and quantization for large language models and vision transformers. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods or require significant computational overhead. Our proposal distinguishes from existing work by focusing on latency-optimized quantization for commodity GPUs, using a kernel-level throughput engineering approach and hardware-faithful evaluation.
Abstract: Deep learning models are increasingly being deployed on commodity GPUs, but their high computational and memory demands can lead to significant inference latency. To address this challenge, we propose a latency-optimized quantization approach that leverages kernel-level throughput engineering and hardware-faithful evaluation. Our approach adapts to the specific hardware characteristics of commodity GPUs, reducing inference latency while maintaining accuracy. We evaluate our approach on a range of deep learning models and demonstrate significant latency reductions, making it an attractive solution for real-time applications.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed latency-optimized quantization approach on a range of deep learning models, including ResNet and MobileNet
Compare the performance of our approach with existing quantization methods, such as SNIP and AMP-ViT
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different kernel-level optimizations on inference latency
Measure the energy consumption and throughput of our approach on commodity GPUs
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may require significant expertise in kernel-level throughput engineering and hardware-faithful evaluation
The approach may not generalize to all types of deep learning models or hardware platforms
The evaluation metrics used may not capture all aspects of system performance
